nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving blackwater rivers, flooded forest margins, and tributary streams of the upper Orinoco basin and the Rio Negro headwaters; associated with submerged roots, overhanging branches, and leaf litter in deep, tannin-stained water

decorPreferences: tall vertical driftwood branches and bogwood to accommodate body depth
dim lighting with floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce) to diffuse light
tannin-stained blackwater (Indian almond leaves, alder cones, peat filtration)
minimal current — gentle sponge filtration preferred
dark substrate (fine black sand) to reduce stress and intensify coloration
no sharp decor edges that could damage extended fins

diet.notes: Insectivore-leaning carnivore in the wild, feeding on small insects, insect larvae, small crustaceans, worms, and small fish. In captivity, accepts high-quality frozen foods: bloodworms, white mosquito larvae, brine shrimp, and mysis shrimp. Live foods (blackworms, daphnia, mosquito larvae) are strongly preferred for conditioning and should be offered regularly. Dry foods (small pellets or flake) are accepted by established individuals but should not be the dietary staple. Feed 2–3 times daily in small quantities; altum angelfish are prone to digestive issues if fed large meals infrequently.

goodWith: conspecifics in groups of 4–6 or as mated pairs in large tanks
dwarf cichlids with similar blackwater requirements (Apistogramma species)
cardinal tetras and rummy-nose tetras — similar water requirements, too large to be eaten by adults
corydoras catfish (blackwater-adapted species such as C. adolfoi)
avoidWith: common or freshwater angelfish (Pterophyllum scalare) — hybridisation risk and disease transfer concern
fast-moving or aggressive fish that outcompete altums for food
fin-nippers (tiger barbs, serpae tetras) — extended fins are a target
small fish that can be eaten (neon tetras, micro rasboras, small shrimp)
hard-water or alkaline-water species incompatible with pH 4.5–6.0
any fish requiring strong water movement

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo for Pterophyllum altum; verify availability and confirm license before use.

summary: The largest Pterophyllum species, reaching 38 cm body depth — roughly 2.5× the size of the common angel. Wild-caught Orinoco/Rio Negro blackwater obligate; demands pH 4.5–6.0 and very soft water. Rarely captive-bred successfully. Specialist-only species commanding high prices and presenting significant care challenges.
careNotes:
Pterophyllum altum is the largest and most demanding of the three angelfish species, and it is fundamentally different from the common P. scalare in its environmental requirements. Adults develop an extreme body depth — up to 38 cm including fin extensions — and require tanks with a minimum internal water column height of 60 cm, more typically 70–80 cm. A footprint of 150 × 60 cm and at least 400 L is considered the minimum for a small group; 600 L+ is preferred.

Water chemistry is non-negotiable. Wild altum angelfish come from the Orinoco basin and upper Rio Negro tributaries where the water is extremely soft (nearly distilled) and acidic, with pH 4.5–6.0, near-zero hardness (gH 0–5 °dH, kH 0–2), and high tannin load. Maintaining these parameters requires RO/DI water remineralised sparingly, regular additions of blackwater extract (Indian almond leaves, alder cones, peat), and very careful monitoring. Fish sourced from dealers who have maintained them at higher pH will need slow, methodical acclimation downward — rapid pH changes are fatal.

Temperature should be held at 27–30 °C; altum angelfish are more warmth-dependent than scalare. Filtration should provide excellent biological turnover with minimal surface turbulence or directional current — sponge filters or large, baffled canister outlets are appropriate.

Altum angelfish are almost entirely wild-caught. Captive breeding attempts have very low success rates outside specialist facilities, owing to the combination of difficult water chemistry, spawning triggers, and fry rearing in near-anoxic blackwater. Expect high shipping mortality; buy only from specialist importers with good track records and acclimation protocols.

This species is frequently confused in the trade with tall-finned P. scalare morphs; true P. altum is distinguishable by its characteristic supraorbital notch (a concave dip above the eye on the forehead profile), a more deeply compressed body, and proportionally more pronounced dorsal and anal fin filament extensions. Genetics and sourcing documentation are the only reliable authentication tools for trade specimens.

Group dynamics: altum angelfish can be maintained in pairs or groups of 4–6 in appropriately sized tanks. Established pairs form strong bonds and can become territorial. Conspecific aggression rises sharply during breeding attempts.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Pterophyllum altum is rarely achieved and is considered advanced even among specialist cichlid breeders. Pairs will sometimes form and spawn on flat vertical surfaces (broad leaves, slate, PVC pipes), but successful egg fertilisation, hatching, and fry rearing is uncommon. Eggs are adhesive and are laid in rows on a cleaned vertical surface. Both parents participate in fanning and guarding, though they frequently eat eggs or fry — particularly in stressful conditions. Fry require infusoria or Paramecium cultures followed by baby brine shrimp; maintaining fry through the first weeks demands water chemistry as stringent as the adults require. Commercial captive-bred specimens available in the trade are vanishingly rare and should be confirmed with DNA or morphological verification, as P. scalare hybrids are sometimes mislabelled.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pterophyllum altum
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provided adult size, water parameters (pH 4.6–6.2, temperature 26–30°C, hardness 0–54 µS), native range (upper Orinoco and Atabapo/Inírida drainages, Venezuela/Colombia), diet, breeding behaviour, biotope description, and species identification notes including the supraorbital notch diagnostic.

additional.name: FishBase — Pterophyllum altum
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), adult size (max TL ~38 cm including fin extensions), native range (Orinoco basin, upper Rio Negro), temperature range, and diet classification (carnivore). Confirmed depthRangeM estimate based on habitat descriptions of flooded forest and slow river margins.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Angelfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Used for comparison of trade expectations versus true altum requirements; confirmed trade confusion with tall-finned P. scalare, importance of species sourcing verification, and general angelfish care norms. Aquarium Co-Op does not maintain a dedicated P. altum page; P. scalare guide used as corroborating reference for family-level care norms.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Pterophyllum altum species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source for trade notes, wild-caught status, body depth measurements, the supraorbital notch identification feature, and the specialist-only advisory context. Confirmed high shipping mortality and rarity of captive-bred specimens.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Pterophyllum altum husbandry
additional.notes: Corroborating specialist source for blackwater parameter requirements, minimum tank height (60 cm internal), breeding difficulty, historical Orinoco collection records, and captive-breeding failure rates. Cross-confirmed water parameters and group-housing recommendations.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is very rarely achieved. Pairs spawn on vertical flat surfaces; eggs are adhesive. Both parents guard spawn but frequently consume eggs or fry, especially when stressed. Fry require infusoria then baby brine shrimp in strictly maintained blackwater conditions. Commercial captive-bred specimens are essentially unavailable; trade supply is almost entirely wild-caught.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Wild-only / rarely captive-bred; demands soft acid (pH 4.5–6.0) blackwater Orinoco conditions, deep tanks (60cm+ height) for adult height (40cm body depth). High shipping mortality. Specialist Apistogramma/discus-tier setup required.
